title: Results
text:
The central question of this paper is whether firms that the government selects for less-competitive settings (Type 1) are actually more cost-efficient than firms that compete only in public auctions (Type 0). The structural model recovers pseudo-costs from observed bids; comparing their distributions reveals whether the government’s discretion is well-placed.


The chart below shows the cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of pseudo-costs for each firm type. A CDF shifted to the left indicates that a firm type has systematically lower costs — a higher share of its firms can complete the project below any given cost threshold.


                            
                                            








NoteHow to read this chart



Each curve shows the share of firms (vertical axis) that have a pseudo-cost below each cost value (horizontal axis). A curve shifted to the left means lower costs — firms of that type are cheaper, on average. Toggle between simple and complex projects to see how the cost advantage reverses.







  
    Simple contracts (no sewage)
    Type 0 has lower costs ✗
    The Type 0 CDF lies to the left of Type 1. Government-selected firms (Type 1) are more expensive for routine pavement work — the 76% of invitation-only contracts without sewage work.
  

  
    Complex contracts (sewage work)
    Type 1 has lower costs ✓
    The Type 1 CDF lies to the left of Type 0. Government-selected firms are genuinely more cost-efficient for projects with sewage — consistent with economies of scope.

title: Results
text:
The central question of this paper is whether firms that the government selects for less-competitive settings (Type 1) are actually more cost-efficient than firms that compete only in public auctions (Type 0). The structural model recovers pseudo-costs from observed bids; comparing their distributions reveals whether the government’s discretion is well-placed.


The chart below shows the cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of pseudo-costs for each firm type. A CDF shifted to the left indicates that a firm type has systematically lower costs — a higher share of its firms can complete the project below any given cost threshold.


                            
                                            








NoteHow to read this chart



Each curve shows the share of firms (vertical axis) that have a pseudo-cost below each cost value (horizontal axis). A curve shifted to the left means lower costs — firms of that type are cheaper, on average. Toggle between simple and complex projects to see how the cost advantage reverses.







  
    Simple contracts (no sewage)
    Type 0 has lower costs ✗
    The Type 0 CDF lies to the left of Type 1. Government-selected firms (Type 1) are more expensive for routine pavement work — the 76% of invitation-only contracts without sewage work.
  

  
    Complex contracts (sewage work)
    Type 1 has lower costs ✓
    The Type 1 CDF lies to the left of Type 0. Government-selected firms are genuinely more cost-efficient for projects with sewage — consistent with economies of scope.

title: Results
text:
Stochastic Frontier Efficiency Index
The structural model recovers pseudo-costs; the SFA model then decomposes these into a frontier cost (the minimum achievable) plus excess cost due to inefficiency. The Battese-Coelli (BC) efficiency index is \(BC = e^{-\hat{\eta}} \in (0,1]\). A value close to 1 means low excess cost; values far from 1 indicate significant inefficiency.


                            
                                            








NoteInterpreting the efficiency index



The BC index is bounded between 0 and 1. A firm with \(BC = 1\) is on the cost frontier — it cannot reduce costs further given its inputs and output. A firm with \(BC = 0.80\) uses 25% more inputs than a frontier firm to produce the same output (\(1/0.80 = 1.25\)). Higher values (further right) indicate less excess cost.



Efficiency Index: Key Numbers
The table below summarizes the efficiency index by firm type (half-normal model, complex projects with sewage):








Statistic
Type 0 (Public-auction-only)
Type 1 (Government-selected)




Mean BC index
Lower
Higher


Interpretation
More excess cost
Less excess cost


Consistent with CDFs?
✓ Yes
✓ Yes



Type 1 firms exhibit higher efficiency indexes on average for complex projects — confirming that the government’s discretion aligns with genuine cost advantages when the contract involves sewage work.

title: Results
text:
Geographic Distribution of Efficiency
The map below shows the average efficiency index by Mexican state, weighted by contract volume. Darker shades indicate lower efficiency (higher excess costs). States are identified by their INEGI codes.


                            
                                            








NoteNotes on geographic variation





State-level efficiency averages reflect the municipalities that conducted public pavement auctions during 2011–2018. Not all states have the same number of contracts, so estimates for sparsely represented states are less precise. The map uses contract-volume-weighted averages to give more weight to states with a higher share of observed contracts.

title: Results
text:
Summary of Main Results








Finding
Simple contracts
Complex contracts




Type 1 pseudo-costs vs. Type 0
Higher (worse)
Lower (better)


Efficiency index (BC), Type 1 vs. Type 0
Lower (worse)
Higher (better)


Policy implication
Use public auctions
Discretion may be justified


Share of I3P contracts
~76%
~24%



The results have a clear policy prescription: for the majority of contracts — small, homogeneous pavement projects without sewage — the government’s selection of firms leads to higher costs. Switching these to public auctions would reduce procurement costs. The exception is complex projects with sewage work, where the selected firms appear to have genuine cost advantages, likely through economies of scope.

title: Political Factors
text: The choice of allocation mechanism is not purely technical — it is also shaped by political incentives. This section documents two political patterns in the data: the electoral cycle and party alignment.

title: Political Factors
text: The choice of allocation mechanism is not purely technical — it is also shaped by political incentives. This section documents two political patterns in the data: the electoral cycle and party alignment.

title: Political Factors
text:
Electoral Cycle
Local governments are more likely to bypass public auctions in the year before municipal elections. The probit model on mechanism choice (public auction = 0, I3P or direct = 1) shows:






ImportantElectoral cycle finding



Being in the year before municipal elections increases the probability of using a discretionary allocation mechanism by 6.2 percentage points, controlling for project size, complexity, and state fixed effects.


This pattern is consistent with electoral-motivated contracting: governments distribute contracts to politically connected firms ahead of elections. The effect is especially pronounced in municipalities governed by the PRI.

Excess Costs Rise Near Elections
The electoral cycle also affects cost efficiency in public auctions — even when the mechanism itself is competitive:

Firm excess costs in public auctions are 13.8% higher in the year before elections
This suggests political pressure distorts bidding behavior even within competitive auctions
One interpretation: connected firms bid less aggressively, expecting future rewards regardless of auction outcomes


  Mechanism:
  
    In election years, firms that expect future direct contracts may shade their bids upward in public auctions — reducing competitive pressure and raising the government's procurement costs. The structural model captures this as higher markup over cost.

title: Political Factors
text:
Party Alignment
Municipal governments that share a party affiliation with the state government face different incentive structures when allocating contracts.






NoteParty alignment result



Municipalities governed by PAN are 18.9 percentage points less likely to use discretionary allocation mechanisms than PRI-governed municipalities, all else equal.


This finding is consistent with institutional explanations: PAN municipalities, at least during 2011–2018, faced stronger internal party pressure toward procedural compliance, or had less access to the political networks that facilitated direct contracting.
Additionally:

When the municipal and state government share a party, firm excess costs fall by approximately 9.8% in public auctions — suggesting that aligned governments may coordinate more efficiently, or face less political pressure to protect connected contractors

title: Political Factors
text:
Probit Results: Mechanism Choice
The table below summarizes the marginal effects from the probit model predicting whether a contract bypasses a public auction (I3P or direct allocation = 1).








Predictor
Marginal Effect
Interpretation




Concrete volume (1 SD increase)
−11.4 pp
Larger projects → more likely to use public auction


Sewage work dummy
−9.8 pp
Complex projects → more likely to use public auction


Election year (year before municipal election)
+6.2 pp
Election pressure → more discretionary allocation


PAN municipality (vs. PRI)
−18.9 pp
PAN → more likely to use public auction



All effects are evaluated at the sample mean. Standard errors clustered at the municipality level.

title: Political Factors
text:
What This Means
The political economy findings complement the cost-efficiency results:

Discretion is selectively misused. Governments bypass auctions more often near elections, when political motivations are strongest. This is precisely when cost-efficiency is least likely to drive the selection.
Even competitive auctions are affected. Higher excess costs in public auctions during election years suggest that the distortions reach beyond the choice of mechanism — they affect competitive behavior within auctions.
Party institutions matter. The large PAN–PRI difference suggests that reducing discretionary contracting may require institutional reforms at the party level, not just legal restrictions on procurement mechanisms.


  Policy Implication
  Procurement reform should target election-period contracting
  The results suggest that a targeted rule — requiring public auctions for simple pavement contracts in the year before elections — could substantially reduce procurement costs without restricting legitimate use of discretion for complex projects.

title: Productivity in Procurement Auctions of Pavement Contracts in Mexico
text:
Daniel Prudencio  · 
    Tecnológico de Monterrey  · 
    Journal of Productivity Analysis, 2025

title: Productivity in Procurement Auctions of Pavement Contracts in Mexico
text:
The Question
When local governments allocate public infrastructure contracts, they can choose between open competition (public auctions) or greater hiring discretion — inviting a handful of firms or directly picking a preferred contractor.
In theory, discretion allows governments to select the most capable firms. In practice, it opens the door to favoritism and corruption. Which is actually happening in Mexico?
This paper answers that question using street pavement contracts in Mexico (2011–2018), combining a structural auction model with stochastic frontier analysis to recover firm-level productivity and compare it across allocation mechanisms.

title: Productivity in Procurement Auctions of Pavement Contracts in Mexico
text:
Key Findings


  
    Complex Contracts
    Government selects the right firms ✓
    For projects that include sewage work, firms selected to settings with less competition have lower costs than firms that only participate in public auctions. Economies of scope likely explain this advantage.
  

  
    Simple Contracts
    Government selects the wrong firms ✗
    For small, simple pavement projects (76% of invitation-only auctions), selected firms have higher costs. These contracts should be put out to public auction.
  

  
    Political Factors
    Electoral cycle drives discretion
    Governments are 6.2% more likely to bypass public auctions in the year before elections. Firm excess costs rise 13.8% near election time in public auctions.

title: Productivity in Procurement Auctions of Pavement Contracts in Mexico
text:
Abstract

When allocating contracts, governments decide between exercising hiring discretion or allowing higher competition without firm selection. Ex-ante, it is not clear which allocation format leads to better outcomes. The trade-off depends in part on the government’s ability to select the best firms when restricting competition and on the probability that this practice leads to corruption. In this paper, I study the allocation of street pavement contracts in Mexico. I combine auction methods with a productivity analysis to indirectly test whether local governments select firms with low excess costs when restricting competition. This indirect approach allows the monitoring of contract allocation in settings with little information on firm reputation. I find that firms selected to settings with less competition have lower costs in complex pavement contracts, but higher costs in simple ones. Contrary to the current practice, the results suggest that the government would benefit from using public auctions for simple pavement contracts.

title: Productivity in Procurement Auctions of Pavement Contracts in Mexico
text:
How to Navigate This Site

Political Factors

Replication
Citation, bibtex, and code

title: Context & Data
text:
Mexico’s Law of Public Works and Related Services requires governments to allocate infrastructure contracts through the procedure that minimizes construction costs. In practice, this means three possible mechanisms:


  
    Mechanism 1
    Public Auction
    Open to all qualified firms. First-price sealed-bid. The law favors this procedure. 617 contracts in 2011–2018.
  

  
    Mechanism 2
    Invitation (I3P)
    Government invites ≥ 3 firms. Used when the project is small or urgent. 2,633 contracts in 2011–2018.
  

  
    Mechanism 3
    Direct Allocation
    Government selects one firm without competition. Allowed under specific legal exceptions. 448 contracts in 2011–2018.
  


The exceptions allowing governments to bypass public auctions are deliberately broad — including vague language about “significant losses” or “force majeure” — giving local officials wide discretion in practice.

title: Context & Data
text:
Mexico’s Law of Public Works and Related Services requires governments to allocate infrastructure contracts through the procedure that minimizes construction costs. In practice, this means three possible mechanisms:


  
    Mechanism 1
    Public Auction
    Open to all qualified firms. First-price sealed-bid. The law favors this procedure. 617 contracts in 2011–2018.
  

  
    Mechanism 2
    Invitation (I3P)
    Government invites ≥ 3 firms. Used when the project is small or urgent. 2,633 contracts in 2011–2018.
  

  
    Mechanism 3
    Direct Allocation
    Government selects one firm without competition. Allowed under specific legal exceptions. 448 contracts in 2011–2018.
  


The exceptions allowing governments to bypass public auctions are deliberately broad — including vague language about “significant losses” or “force majeure” — giving local officials wide discretion in practice.

title: Context & Data
text:
The Two Types of Firms
The key distinction in this study is between firms based on their history with the government:

Type 1 — “Government-selected”: firms that have received at least one contract by direct allocation or been invited to an I3P auction. More experienced, more politically connected.
Type 0 — “Public-auction-only”: firms that have only ever competed in open public auctions. Less experience, no special relationship with the government.

The central question: are Type 1 firms actually more cost-efficient, or are they just better connected?

title: Context & Data
text:
Bid Data: Public Procurement Auctions
The analysis focuses on the 617 public auction contracts for which full bid data are available (2,784 individual bids after removing contracts missing project characteristics).



Statistic
Value




Average bid (thousands MXN per m³ concrete, 2018 prices)
9.01


Average winning bid
9.40


Std. deviation of bids
7.57


Average number of bidders per auction
6.9


— of which Type 1 (government-selected)
2.0


— of which Type 0 (public-auction-only)
4.9


Average concrete volume per contract (m³)
730.8


Share of contracts with sewage work
38%


Average project duration (months)
3.4




Note on project size: The average project paves 8–9 blocks (50 m each), using hydraulic concrete — a relatively homogeneous, small-scale construction task. This homogeneity is intentional: it reduces unobserved heterogeneity in the structural model.

title: Context & Data
section: Evidence of Asymmetry Between Firm Types
text:
Evidence of Asymmetry Between Firm Types
Before structural estimation, simple reduced-form evidence already reveals important differences between firm types:
Bidding behavior: Type 1 firms bid on average 8.5% higher than Type 0 firms in the same auction, even after controlling for project characteristics. This is consistent with Type 1 firms having lower costs but shading bids upward, or alternatively, being selected for non-cost reasons.
Experience gap: Type 1 firms competed in an average of 6.6 contracts (all mechanisms) versus only 3.4 for Type 0 firms. In public auctions alone, Type 1 firms averaged 4.5 auctions vs. 3.4 for Type 0 firms.
Bid distributions: A Kolmogorov-Smirnov test rejects equality of bid distributions at the 25th and 75th percentiles of project size.

title: Context & Data
text:
Estimated Cost Distributions
The chart below shows the distributions of pseudo-costs (bids minus the markup implied by the structural auction model) for each firm type in public auctions. A distribution shifted to the left indicates lower costs.


                            
                                            








NoteHow are pseudo-costs estimated?





The observed bids are not firms’ costs — they include a strategic markup. The structural auction model (explained in Methods) recovers each firm’s underlying cost from its bid, using the equilibrium relationship between bidding strategies and cost distributions. These recovered values are called pseudo-costs.

title: Context & Data
text:
Choice of Allocation Mechanism
A probit model on the choice of mechanism (public auction = 0, direct/I3P = 1) reveals that:

Project size: A one-standard-deviation increase in concrete volume reduces the probability of bypassing a public auction by 11.4 percentage points. Smaller projects are more likely to use discretionary mechanisms.
Sewage work: Projects with sewage work are 9.8 pp less likely to be directly allocated or invited — complex projects go to public auction more often.
Election year: Being in the year before municipal elections increases the probability of bypassing a public auction by 6.2 pp.
Party (PAN vs. PRI): PAN-governed municipalities are 18.9 pp less likely to use discretionary mechanisms than PRI municipalities.

title: Methods
text: This paper combines two econometric approaches that are rarely used together: a structural procurement auction model to recover firm costs from observed bids, and a stochastic frontier analysis (SFA) to measure how efficiently each firm uses inputs. The diagram below summarizes the two-step procedure.

title: Methods
text:
Step 1: Structural Procurement Auction Model

Setup
The government runs a first-price sealed-bid procurement auction. There are \(n\) risk-neutral bidders, of two types:

Type 1 (\(n_1\) bidders): firms that have at some point received a direct contract or I3P invitation
Type 0 (\(n_0\) bidders): firms that have only participated in public auctions, with \(n_0 + n_1 = n\)

Each firm \(i\) of type \(j \in \{0, 1\}\) has a private cost \(c_{ji}\) drawn independently from \(F_j(\cdot)\), defined on \([\underline{c}, \bar{c}]\). Costs are independent private values (IPV) — a natural assumption for small, homogeneous pavement projects where a common value component is unlikely.


Equilibrium and Identification
At Bayesian Nash Equilibrium, each bidder chooses a bid to maximize expected profit. For a Type 1 firm:
\[b_{1i} - c_{1i} = \frac{1}{(n_1 - 1)\,\frac{g_1(b_{1i})}{1 - G_1(b_{1i})} + n_0\,\frac{g_0(b_{1i})}{1 - G_0(b_{1i})}}\]
where \(G_j(\cdot)\) and \(g_j(\cdot)\) are the bid CDF and PDF of type \(j\) firms. A symmetric equation holds for Type 0. This is the GPV inversion formula (Guerre, Perrigne & Vuong 2000), extended to asymmetric bidders by Flambard & Perrigne (2006).






NoteThe key insight



The equilibrium condition directly maps observed bids to unobserved costs — without needing to know the equilibrium strategies explicitly. The bid distributions \(G_0(\cdot)\) and \(G_1(\cdot)\) are estimated nonparametrically from the data.




Estimation
The estimation proceeds in three nonparametric steps:

Estimate bid distributions \(\hat{G}_j(\cdot)\) and \(\hat{g}_j(\cdot)\) using kernel density estimators, conditioning on project characteristics \(\mathbf{z}\) (concrete volume, sewage dummy)
Recover pseudo-costs \(\hat{c}_{ji}\) by plugging estimated bid densities into the inversion formula above
Estimate cost distributions \(\hat{f}_j(\cdot)\) from the recovered pseudo-costs

Practical details: a triweight kernel is used for continuous variables; a smoothed binary kernel for the sewage dummy; 10% boundary trimming is applied; and bids are log-transformed to reduce skewness.

title: Methods
text:
Step 2: Stochastic Frontier Analysis

The Cost Frontier
Given a level of output \(z\) (cubic meters of concrete) and input prices \(w\) (concrete price, construction wages, machinery rental), each firm minimizes cost subject to a production constraint. Allowing for technical inefficiency \(\eta \geq 0\), the firm’s actual cost relates to the optimal (frontier) cost by:
\[C^a = C^*(w, z) \cdot e^\eta\]
where \(\eta\) is the excess cost — the proportional amount by which the firm overuses all inputs relative to the efficient frontier. A firm with \(\eta = 0\) is fully efficient; \(\eta > 0\) means it could produce the same output with fewer inputs.


The SFA Model
Taking logs and adding a noise term \(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)\):
\[\ln C^a = \ln C^*(w, z) + \eta + \varepsilon\]
The inefficiency term \(\eta \geq 0\) is estimated under two distributional assumptions (for robustness):



Model
Distribution of \(\eta\)




Half-normal
\(\eta \sim \lvert \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2) \rvert\)


Truncated-normal
\(\eta \sim \mathcal{N}^+(\mu, \sigma_u^2)\)



The efficiency index reported in Results is \(BC = e^{-\eta}\), bounded between 0 and 1. A value close to 1 means the firm is near the cost frontier (low excess cost); values further from 1 indicate greater inefficiency.


Connection Between Steps 1 and 2
The pseudo-costs \(\hat{c}_{ji}\) from Step 1 serve as the dependent variable \(C^a\) in the SFA. This is the methodological novelty of the paper: auction methods provide the cost data that SFA requires but typically cannot observe directly.
By comparing the efficiency index distributions of Type 1 and Type 0 firms, we can test whether the government’s discretionary selection of firms corresponds to genuine cost advantages.

title: Replication

title: Replication
Replication
text:
Data and Code

Data Sources
The analysis uses administrative procurement data from Mexico’s CompraNet platform (Mexico’s public procurement registry), covering pavement contracts in the state of Jalisco from 2011 to 2018.



Dataset
Description
Use




data_final.csv
Bid-level data with pseudo-costs (c) and bid values (f_c) by firm type (typeb)
Cost density chart in Context & Data


data_densities.csv
CDF of pseudo-costs at project size quantiles, simple projects
Cost CDF chart in Results


data_densities_drenaje.csv
CDF of pseudo-costs at project size quantiles, complex projects
Cost CDF chart in Results


efficiency_index_comparison_drenaje_sfa.csv
Firm-level BC efficiency index (bc_h) by type
Efficiency density chart in Results


efficiency_index_sfa_drenaje.csv
State-level weighted efficiency index (bc_t_weighted_ent)
Mexico map in Results





Methodological Code
The structural auction model is estimated in R using custom kernel density estimation routines. Pseudo-costs are recovered from observed bids using the GPV inversion formula, with:

Triweight kernel for continuous project characteristics (concrete volume)
Smoothed binary kernel for the sewage dummy
10% boundary trimming to reduce kernel edge bias
Log-transformation of bids to reduce skewness

The stochastic frontier analysis is estimated using maximum likelihood, with two distributional assumptions for the inefficiency term (half-normal and truncated-normal).


This Website
This website was built with Quarto as a multi-page HTML site. All charts are rendered in Python using plotly and scipy. The source .qmd files are in Quarto/Paper03_ProcurementAuctions/.
